docs.title: calemdar
docs.text: Recurring-event manager for Obsidian Full Calendar. Markdown as source of truth, server-expanded occurrences.

docs.title: What it is
docs.text: calemdar (calendar + md) is a small Go daemon plus CLI that turns recurring events in an Obsidian vault into flat, per-date markdown files that Obsidian's Full Calendar plugin can read without surprises.

docs.title: Why
docs.text:
Full Calendar has a long-standing footgun: drag a single occurrence of a recurring event to a new slot and the plugin overwrites the recurrence rule, wiping every other occurrence in the series.

calemdar sidesteps the footgun entirely. It keeps recurrence rules in server-private root files under recurring/ and materialises each occurrence as an independent single-event file under events/. Full Calendar sees only flat events, so drag, drop, and edit are all safe.

docs.title: How it works in one diagram
docs.text:
recurring/workout.md ──▶  reconcile  ──▶  events/health/2026-05-03-workout.md
   (recurrence rule)                        events/health/2026-05-05-workout.md
                                            events/health/2026-05-07-workout.md
                                            ...
                                             │
                                             └─▶ Full Calendar reads these

When you drag an expanded event in Obsidian, the daemon notices the change, flips user-owned: true on that file, and leaves it alone forever. The rest of the series keeps regenerating from the root.

docs.title: Where to next
docs.text:
• Quickstart — from zero to running daemon in five commands.
  • Architecture — reactor, reconcile, watcher, store.
  • Configuration — every config key.
  • CLI — every subcommand.
  • Schema — frontmatter shapes for roots and events.
  • Design — folder layout + server behaviour.
  • FAQ — common questions.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: v1 shipped. Single-host, single-timezone (Europe/Stockholm), single-vault. Notifications land in-daemon via ntfy — see configuration. ICS export stays deferred.

docs.title: License
docs.text: MIT. See LICENSE.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: calemdar is a single static Go binary. One process, one vault, one host. The internals split into a small set of cooperating packages.

docs.title: Source-of-truth layering
docs.text:
1. Markdown files on disk — the only source of truth. Recurring roots in recurring/<slug>.md, expanded events in events/<calendar>/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<slug>.md.
  2. SQLite cache at .calemdar/cache.db — a projection of the markdown, rebuildable at any time via calemdar reindex. Fast lookups for the watcher, reactor, and CLI listers.
  3. In-memory config populated once at startup from config.yaml.

If you delete the cache the daemon rebuilds it on next start. If you delete a markdown file the daemon notices and reconciles accordingly.

docs.title: internal/watch — filesystem watcher
docs.text:
Wraps fsnotify with recursive-directory walking and a debounce layer. Coalesces bursts of events on the same path within debounce_ms (default 500ms) so a single Obsidian save doesn't trigger a reconcile storm.

Also suppresses self-writes: each time the writer touches a file it records the path + mtime in the store. When a matching event comes back from fsnotify the watcher swallows it, preventing feedback loops.

docs.title: internal/reactor — Full Calendar translator
docs.text:
Watches events/ for files whose frontmatter carries Full Calendar's recurrence shape (type: recurring or type: rrule). When one appears, reactor:

  1. Reads the FC frontmatter.
  2. Builds an equivalent calemdar root (freq, interval, byday / bymonthday).
  3. Writes it to recurring/<slug>.md.
  4. Deletes the FC-authored source file.
  5. Hands the new root to reconcile for immediate expansion.

End result: the user does the FC-native "new recurring event" gesture and calemdar converts it to its own shape without requiring the user to learn the CLI.

Rejects v1-unsupported rules (positional BYDAY, COUNT=, etc.) with a clear error rather than silently losing data.

docs.title: internal/reconcile — root → occurrences
docs.text:
Given a root, computes the occurrence set for the window [today, today + horizon_months], respecting freq, interval, byday, bymonthday, start-date, until, and exceptions. Then:

  • For each target date, check if the event file exists.
  • If it exists and user-owned: true → skip.
  • If it exists and user-owned: false → overwrite (future only).
  • If it does not exist → create.
  • Orphan sweep: any existing event with this series-id that is NOT in the target set, NOT user-owned, and has date >= today → delete.
  • Past is immutable. reconcile never touches an event whose date is earlier than today, regardless of user-owned.

Reconcile is idempotent; running it twice in a row does nothing the second time.

docs.title: internal/autoown — user-owned flag flipper
docs.text: Watches events/. When an event file is modified and the write was NOT from the server (self-write suppression), autoown reads the file, sets user-owned: true, and writes it back. After that point, reconcile leaves it alone forever.

docs.title: internal/store — SQLite cache
docs.text:
Pure-Go SQLite (modernc.org/sqlite, no CGO). Two tables, series and occurrences — see design for the schema. Exposes:

  • Open(vault) — open or create the cache.
  • Reindex(vault) — scan disk and rebuild from scratch.
  • Lookup helpers for the watcher and CLI listers.
  • Self-write tracking (path + mtime) for fsnotify feedback suppression.

docs.title: internal/writer — atomic markdown writes
docs.text: Writes expanded events via write-to-temp + rename. Records the mtime in the store so the watcher's self-write suppressor can identify its own events. Uses 0644 on files and 0755 on intermediate directories.

docs.title: internal/vault — path resolution
docs.text: Resolves the vault root (tilde expansion, absolute path normalisation) and scaffolds the directory tree (recurring/, archive/, events/<cal>/).

docs.title: internal/serve — daemon glue
docs.text: Wires watcher → dispatcher → reactor / autoown / reconcile. Owns the nightly timer (extend + archive at nightly_at). Stops cleanly on SIGINT / SIGTERM.

docs.title: Create a new recurring series via the CLI
docs.text:
calemdar series new
  └─▶ prompt.go gathers fields
  └─▶ writer.Write(root) → recurring/<slug>.md
  └─▶ watcher sees it
      └─▶ dispatch to reconcile.Series
          └─▶ writer.Write(...) × N → events/<cal>/<date>-<slug>.md
          └─▶ store updates occurrences table

docs.title: Drag an occurrence in Obsidian
docs.text:
obsidian writes events/<cal>/<date>-<slug>.md with new date / time
  └─▶ watcher sees it (NOT a self-write)
      └─▶ dispatch to autoown
          └─▶ writer.Write(<same path>) with user-owned: true
  └─▶ reconcile of the root later sees user-owned, skips this file forever

docs.title: Nightly pass
docs.text:
03:00 local (timezone-aware)
  └─▶ for each root: reconcile.Series (extends horizon by ~1 day)
  └─▶ archive.Run (moves events past archive_cutoff_months)

docs.title: Concurrency model
docs.text:
• Exactly one calemdar serve process per vault. A lease file in the store directory prevents two daemons from fighting.
  • Watcher events are serialised through the dispatcher — reconcile, reactor, and autoown never run concurrently on the same file.
  • CLI maintenance commands (reindex, extend, archive) are safe to run while the daemon is up; they share the store and contend on SQLite transactions.

docs.title: internal/notify — ntfy push
docs.text: When notifications.enabled is true, serve spawns a goroutine that ticks every 60 seconds, queries the store for upcoming events, and POSTs to <ntfy_url>/<ntfy_topic> for each event crossing a configured lead-minute window. All-day events are skipped. Optional per-calendar filter via notifications.calendars. The calemdar notify test subcommand sends a one-shot test push regardless of enabled.

docs.title: What calemdar does NOT do
docs.text:
• No CalDAV, no ICS. If you want those, run Radicale beside it.
  • No multi-device daemon. The vault is Syncthing-friendly, but exactly one device runs serve.

docs.title: CLI reference
docs.text: Every calemdar subcommand, grouped by purpose. Run calemdar --help or calemdar <command> --help for the built-in version.

docs.title: Global flags
docs.text:
--vault <path>    override vault (config + env still ignored when set)

Vault resolution: --vault flag → $CALEMDAR_VAULT → vault: in config → error.

docs.title: calemdar serve
docs.text:
Run the long-lived daemon: filesystem watcher plus nightly timers. Reacts to changes in recurring/ and events/ live, and runs reconcile + archive on the schedule defined by nightly_at.

calemdar serve

Usually invoked from the systemd user unit — see examples/calemdar.service.

docs.title: One-shot maintenance
docs.text: These all do what the daemon does continuously, but once. Safe to run while serve is also running; they share the same vault state.

docs.title: calemdar setup
docs.text:
Create the calendar subfolders under the vault (or under base_path). Idempotent. The daemon does this at startup too.

calemdar setup

docs.title: calemdar reindex
docs.text:
Rebuild the SQLite cache from disk. The cache is a projection, not a source of truth — delete it and reindex will put it back exactly as it was.

calemdar reindex

docs.title: calemdar reactor
docs.text:
One-shot scan of events/ for Full-Calendar-authored recurring events (events that still carry FC's recurrence frontmatter). Each found event is translated into a root under recurring/ and expanded into flat occurrences.

calemdar reactor

You rarely need to run this by hand — the daemon runs it on startup and reacts to new FC recurring events live.

docs.title: calemdar extend
docs.text:
Reconcile every recurring series, extending each to the configured horizon (horizon_months, default 12). Runs nightly inside the daemon; useful to invoke after a horizon bump.

calemdar extend

docs.title: calemdar expand <id-or-slug>
docs.text:
Reconcile a single series. Identify the series by either its UUIDv7 id field or the current filename slug (without .md).

calemdar expand workout
calemdar expand 019073c4-d7e0-7d8f-a1f3-8b2c9e5f4a10

docs.title: calemdar archive
docs.text:
Move events older than archive_cutoff_months (default 6) into archive/. Filenames are preserved; directory structure mirrors events/ but rooted at archive/.

calemdar archive

docs.title: calemdar series new
docs.text:
Interactive prompt-driven recurring root creation. Writes the new root to recurring/<slug>.md and immediately expands it.

calemdar series new

docs.title: calemdar series list
docs.text:
Tabular listing of all recurring series.

calemdar series list

Columns: slug, calendar, title, freq, interval, start date, until date.

docs.title: calemdar series show <id-or-slug>
docs.text:
Detailed view of one series — every frontmatter field plus the file path.

calemdar series show workout

docs.title: calemdar series except <id-or-slug> <date>
docs.text:
Add a date to the series' exceptions list and reconcile. The occurrence on that date is dropped (or swept if already materialised and not user-owned).

calemdar series except workout 2026-06-24

Warns if <date> is in the past — past occurrences are immutable, so the exception will have no effect on them.

docs.title: calemdar event new
docs.text:
Interactive prompt-driven one-off event creation. Writes directly under events/<calendar>/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<slug>.md with no series-id — the daemon will not touch it.

calemdar event new

docs.title: calemdar event list [--range=<range>]
docs.text:
List events in a date range.

calemdar event list --range=today
calemdar event list --range=week       # default
calemdar event list --range=month
calemdar event list --range=all

docs.title: calemdar event show <path>
docs.text:
Detailed view of one event. Path can be absolute or relative to the vault.

calemdar event show events/health/2026-05-03-workout.md

docs.title: Notifications
docs.text: Configured via the notifications: block in config.yaml. When enabled, the serve daemon pushes upcoming events to an ntfy topic; these subcommands help set up and verify that path.

docs.title: calemdar notify test
docs.text:
Send a single test push to the configured ntfy topic. Does NOT gate on notifications.enabled — the point is to prove the URL + topic work before flipping the daemon switch on.

calemdar notify test

Errors if ntfy_url or ntfy_topic are missing.

docs.title: calemdar config path
docs.text:
Print the config file lookup path (does not check existence).

calemdar config path

docs.title: calemdar config show
docs.text:
Print the active config — defaults merged with the file on disk. If no file exists, prints defaults with a note at the top.

calemdar config show

docs.title: calemdar config init [--override]
docs.text:
Write a default config file. Errors if one already exists, unless --override is passed. Drops into $EDITOR after writing if $EDITOR is set.

calemdar config init
calemdar config init --override    # overwrite an existing file

docs.title: calemdar config edit
docs.text:
Open the config in $EDITOR and validate on save. If the file does not exist, a default stub is written first. Invalid YAML or out-of-range values print diagnostics and leave the in-process config unchanged.

calemdar config edit

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: calemdar reads a YAML config from the XDG config directory, defaulting to ~/.config/calemdar/config.yaml. Every field is optional; missing keys fall back to the defaults documented below.

docs.title: Where the file lives
docs.text:
calemdar config path     # print the lookup path
calemdar config show     # print the active (post-merge) values
calemdar config init     # write a default config (errors if one exists)
calemdar config edit     # open in $EDITOR, validate on save

Resolution order, highest wins:

  1. --vault flag (vault path only)
  2. $CALEMDAR_VAULT environment variable (vault path only)
  3. vault: key in the config file
  4. Built-in defaults (for non-vault keys)

docs.title: vault
docs.text:
Type: string (path) Default: none — required for the daemon

Absolute path to your Obsidian vault. ~/ is expanded.

vault: /home/you/obsidian/my-vault

You can also leave this empty in the file and supply it via --vault or $CALEMDAR_VAULT.

docs.title: base_path
docs.text:
Type: string (relative path) Default: "" (vault root)

Optional subfolder inside the vault under which calemdar's tree lives. Empty means directly under the vault root. Useful when your vault already has top-level structure and you want calendar data tucked away.

base_path: "02 - Personal/Calendar"

Must stay inside the vault; .. segments are rejected.

docs.title: timezone
docs.text:
Type: IANA timezone string Default: Europe/Stockholm

Used for "today" resolution and the nightly timer. v1 is effectively single-timezone — every event is stored in this zone and there is no per-event override.

timezone: Europe/Stockholm

docs.title: nightly_at
docs.text:
Type: HH:MM string (24h, local to timezone) Default: 03:00

When the nightly reconcile + archive pass runs.

nightly_at: "03:00"

docs.title: horizon_months
docs.text:
Type: integer (1–120) Default: 12

How many months ahead of today to keep events materialised. The nightly timer extends this window as days pass.

horizon_months: 12

docs.title: archive_cutoff_months
docs.text:
Type: integer (0–120) Default: 6

Events older than (today - this many months) are moved into archive/ during the nightly pass. Set to 0 to archive everything past.

archive_cutoff_months: 6

docs.title: debounce_ms
docs.text:
Type: integer (1–60000) Default: 500

Filesystem-event coalesce window in milliseconds. Multiple writes to the same path within the window collapse into a single reconcile. Longer values are more efficient; shorter values respond faster.

debounce_ms: 500

docs.title: calendars
docs.text:
Type: list of strings Default: [health, tech, work, life, friends-family, special]

The calendar subfolders under events/. Each is a Full Calendar source with its own colour. Adding to the list requires a Full Calendar settings update and usually a re-scaffold (calemdar setup).

calendars:
  - health
  - tech
  - work
  - life
  - friends-family
  - special

docs.title: notifications
docs.text:
Upcoming-event push notifications via ntfy. When enabled, the serve daemon runs a 60-second ticker that queries the store for upcoming events and POSTs to <ntfy_url>/<ntfy_topic> when a start time crosses one of the configured lead-minute windows. All-day events are skipped (no natural trigger time).

notifications:
  enabled: true
  ntfy_url: https://ntfy.sh
  ntfy_topic: my-private-calemdar-topic
  lead_minutes: [5, 60]   # default
  calendars: []           # empty = all calendars

docs.title: notifications.enabled
docs.text:
Type: boolean Default: false

Switch. When false, the daemon does nothing ntfy-related regardless of the other fields.

docs.title: notifications.ntfy_url
docs.text:
Type: string (URL) Default: none — required when enabled

Base URL of the ntfy server. Public https://ntfy.sh works; self-hosted instances work too.

docs.title: notifications.ntfy_topic
docs.text:
Type: string Default: none — required when enabled

Topic name appended to ntfy_url. Keep it unguessable if the server is public — anyone with the topic name can read and write.

docs.title: notifications.lead_minutes
docs.text:
Type: list of positive integers Default: [5, 60]

Minutes before an event to push. Each value produces its own notification. [5, 60] pushes once an hour out and once five minutes out.

docs.title: notifications.calendars
docs.text:
Type: list of strings Default: [] (all calendars)

Restrict notifications to events in these calendars. Empty means all.

You can verify configuration end-to-end without waiting for a real event:

calemdar notify test

This POSTs a single test push and exits. It does NOT gate on enabled, so you can confirm URL + topic before flipping the switch on the daemon.

docs.title: Full example
docs.text:
vault: /home/you/obsidian/my-vault
base_path: ""
timezone: Europe/Stockholm
nightly_at: "03:00"
horizon_months: 12
archive_cutoff_months: 6
debounce_ms: 500
calendars:
  - health
  - tech
  - work
  - life
  - friends-family
  - special
notifications:
  enabled: false
  # ntfy_url: https://ntfy.sh
  # ntfy_topic: my-private-calemdar-topic
  # lead_minutes: [5, 60]
  # calendars: []

See also examples/config.yaml in the repo.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
calemdar config edit re-validates on save. Any invalid field prints a diagnostic and leaves Active unchanged so the daemon keeps the last good values. You can also validate any time with:

calemdar config show    # prints the merged config or the parse error

docs.title: calemdar — design
docs.text: Status: v1 shipped. All CLI subcommands listed below are implemented and covered by tests / smoke runs. Deferred items (notifications, ICS) are explicitly called out as such.

docs.title: Folder layout (in the Obsidian vault)
docs.text:
<vault>/
├── events/                      # FC reads these. one source per subfolder = one color
│   ├── health/                  # flat — FC does NOT recurse into subfolders
│   │   ├── 2026-05-03-workout.md
│   │   └── 2026-05-05-meds-refill.md
│   ├── tech/
│   ├── work/
│   ├── life/
│   ├── friends-family/
│   └── special/
├── recurring/                   # NOT an FC source. server's source of truth
│   ├── workout.md
│   └── monday-standup.md
└── archive/                     # NOT an FC source. past events >6 months old
    └── 2025/
        ├── health/
        └── tech/

docs.title: Why this shape
docs.text:
• Six FC sources, one per calendar → each gets its own color without any per-event color property.
  • Events flat under each calendar → Full Calendar's local-folder source does not recurse. Year subfolders hide events from the index. Filenames are date-prefixed so sort-by-name is chronological.
  • recurring/ outside events/ → FC never sees the root templates.
  • archive/ outside events/ → FC never indexes old data. Full-text search and grep still work for the human.

docs.title: Server behavior
docs.text: The server has two jobs: expand recurring roots into concrete occurrences, and archive old occurrences. It runs as a daemon (calemdar serve) or via one-shot CLI commands.

docs.title: Expansion
docs.text:
• Watch recurring/ and events/ via fsnotify. Debounce 500ms to coalesce burst writes.
  • On root add/change:
  • Parse the root's frontmatter.
  • Compute the set of occurrence dates for the window [today, today + 12 months], respecting freq, interval, byday/bymonthday, start-date, until, and exceptions.
  • For each date in the set:
    • Compute target path: events/<calendar>/<year>/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<slug>.md.
    • If target exists AND user-owned: true → skip.
    • If target exists AND user-owned: false → overwrite (future only; see below).
    • If target does not exist → create.
  • Orphan sweep: for any existing expanded event with this series-id that is NOT in the computed set AND has user-owned: false AND date >= today → delete. (User-owned occurrences are preserved even when orphaned from the series — they stand on their own.)
  • Past is immutable. The server never touches any event where date < today, regardless of user-owned status.

docs.title: User-ownership detection
docs.text:
• Each expanded event has user-owned: false when first written.
  • The server watches events/ via fsnotify. When an events/... file is modified AND the modification was not from the server itself, the server reads the file, sets user-owned: true, and writes it back.
  • Detecting "modification was from the server itself" is done by comparing the mtime to the server's own last-write timestamp (tracked in the SQLite cache).
  • After user-owned: true, the server never regenerates this occurrence.

docs.title: Horizon extension
docs.text:
• Nightly timer (default 03:00 local). For each active root, extend the window by adding any new occurrences that now fall inside [today, today + 12 months].
  • Idempotent. Safe to run ad-hoc via calemdar extend.

docs.title: Archive
docs.text:
• Nightly timer. For any expanded event where date < today - 6 months: move to archive/<year>/<calendar>/<filename>.
  • Filenames preserved. Directory structure mirrors events/ but rooted at archive/.
  • Idempotent. Safe to run ad-hoc via calemdar archive.

docs.title: SQLite cache
docs.text: Not a source of truth. Rebuilt from recurring/ + events/ on calemdar reindex. The cache exists to make lookups fast. If deleted, the server rebuilds it.

docs.title: Schema (sketch)
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE series (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,             -- UUIDv7
  slug TEXT NOT NULL,              -- current filename (without .md)
  calendar TEXT NOT NULL,
  title TEXT NOT NULL,
  freq TEXT NOT NULL,
  interval INTEGER NOT NULL,
  byday TEXT,                      -- JSON array
  bymonthday TEXT,                 -- JSON array
  start_date TEXT NOT NULL,        -- YYYY-MM-DD
  until_date TEXT,                 -- YYYY-MM-DD, nullable
  start_time TEXT,                 -- HH:MM, nullable for all-day
  end_time TEXT,
  all_day INTEGER NOT NULL,        -- 0 or 1
  exceptions TEXT,                 -- JSON array
  root_path TEXT NOT NULL,         -- relative path for quick lookup
  root_mtime INTEGER NOT NULL      -- unix seconds
);

CREATE TABLE occurrences (
  path TEXT PRIMARY KEY,           -- relative path from vault root
  series_id TEXT,                  -- nullable for one-offs
  date TEXT NOT NULL,              -- YYYY-MM-DD
  calendar TEXT NOT NULL,
  user_owned INTEGER NOT NULL,
  expanded_at TEXT NOT NULL,       -- RFC 3339
  server_last_write INTEGER NOT NULL, -- unix seconds
  FOREIGN KEY (series_id) REFERENCES series(id)
);

CREATE INDEX occurrences_date ON occurrences(date);
CREATE INDEX occurrences_series ON occurrences(series_id);

docs.title: CLI surface (v1)
docs.text:
calemdar serve                     # run the watcher + nightly timers
calemdar reindex                   # rebuild SQLite from disk
calemdar expand <series-id>        # force-expand a single series
calemdar extend                    # extend the 12mo horizon for all series
calemdar archive                   # archive events older than 6 months

calemdar event new                 # create a one-off event interactively
calemdar event list [--range=today|week|month|...]
calemdar event show <path>

calemdar series new                # create a recurring root interactively
calemdar series list
calemdar series show <id-or-slug>
calemdar series except <id> <date> # add to exceptions list

docs.title: Concurrency / sync
docs.text:
• The vault is Syncthing-synced. Multiple devices may write.
  • Only one device runs the calemdar serve daemon at a time. Running two daemons against the same vault is unsupported (lease file in SQLite dir).
  • Obsidian and the server will not fight over individual event files: obsidian writes to events/... freely, the server only writes to events/... during expansion. On human edit, the server notices and sets user-owned: true — it does not fight the human.
  • .sync-conflict-* files: if they appear, surface in logs, don't delete.

docs.title: Language + runtime
docs.text:
• Go. Single static binary. Embeds daemon + CLI. Low memory, fast startup, fsnotify stdlib-adjacent, modernc.org/sqlite for pure-Go SQLite, no CGO.
  • Binary name: calemdar. Installed via go install or just install.
  • Hosting: laptop for v1. Architecture is server-ready — move to the home server when notifications land.

docs.title: Notifications (deferred)
docs.text:
• Not in v1.
  • When added: a separate calemdar-notify binary reads the SQLite cache for upcoming events in the next N minutes and pushes via ntfy (user's existing channel). Runs via systemd --user timer, NOT the main daemon.
  • No browser notifications. No per-event config. One ntfy topic, one lead-time setting per calendar.

docs.title: ICS export (deferred, maybe-never)
docs.text:
• Not in v1.
  • If ever added: a read-only ICS projection of events/ + expanded recurring series. Served via tiny HTTP handler in calemdar serve.
  • CalDAV is out of scope. If the user ever wants CalDAV, deploy Radicale and point Obsidian at it — don't reinvent.

docs.title: Why is the past immutable?
docs.text:
Two reasons:

  1. Log integrity. If you edited a root a year ago and reconcile retroactively rewrote last year's occurrences to match, you would lose the history of what actually happened. Calendar entries double as a journal — a workout that happened on Tuesday belongs on Tuesday even if the recurring rule has since moved to Wednesday.
  2. User-ownership consistency. Past occurrences often carry notes, attendees, or post-hoc edits. Regenerating them would clobber that work silently. Making past immutable regardless of user-owned means you never have to reason about which flag a year-old event has.

If you really want to rewrite the past, edit the markdown file in Obsidian directly. The server treats that as a human edit and lets it stand.

docs.title: What happens if the daemon is off?
docs.text:
Obsidian and Full Calendar keep working. Drags and edits happen as normal. When you start the daemon again:

  • Any FC-authored recurring events created while it was off are caught by the reactor on startup.
  • Any drags that happened while it was off are caught by autoown on the next write to those files (or via calemdar reindex — a reindex normalises user-owned based on mtime vs the store's last recorded write).
  • Reconcile runs to close the horizon gap for each series.

There is no per-hour state the daemon carries in memory that can't be rebuilt from disk. Missed wake-ups are fine.

docs.title: How do I migrate from a different folder layout?
docs.text:
Short version: move the markdown files into the layout calemdar wants, then run calemdar reindex.

Longer version, step by step:

  1. Back up the vault.
  2. Stop the daemon: systemctl --user stop calemdar (or Ctrl-C).
  3. Move existing per-date event files into events/<calendar>/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<slug>.md (one folder per calendar). Filenames MUST start with YYYY-MM-DD-.
  4. If you have recurring rules in Full Calendar format already, leave them where Full Calendar wrote them — the reactor will translate them on first run.
  5. If you want to write calemdar-native roots by hand, drop them in recurring/<slug>.md matching the schema.
  6. Run calemdar reindex to rebuild the cache.
  7. Run calemdar extend to expand all roots to the full horizon.
  8. Start the daemon.

If you have a pile of legacy events with inconsistent naming, it is usually faster to rename them with a short shell script (mv "$f" "$(date -d ... +%Y-%m-%d)-$slug.md") than to teach calemdar to parse alternatives.

docs.title: Can I install via AUR / Homebrew?
docs.text:
Not yet packaged. For now:

go install github.com/arch-err/calemdar/cmd/calemdar@latest

Release binaries are published via goreleaser on tagged versions — see releases. AUR and Homebrew formulae may land if there's demand.

docs.title: Why a separate recurring/ folder? Why not put roots next to events?
docs.text:
Full Calendar reads every .md under a configured source folder. If a recurring root lived in events/<calendar>/, Full Calendar would render it as an event (usually on the wrong date, or as an all-day), which is worse than useless.

Putting roots in recurring/ — which is NOT a Full Calendar source — keeps them invisible to the plugin while still being plain markdown editable from anywhere in Obsidian.

docs.title: Why modernc.org/sqlite instead of mattn/go-sqlite3?
docs.text: No CGO. Clean cross-compile. Static binary. Good enough performance for the cache sizes calemdar deals with (tens of thousands of events at worst).

docs.title: Can I run two daemons?
docs.text:
No. A lease file in the SQLite cache directory prevents it. The vault is Syncthing-friendly — run the daemon on one host and let the vault sync normally. If you run the daemon on your laptop but open Obsidian on your phone, the phone only reads; all writes get picked up by the laptop daemon when the sync lands.

If you try to start a second daemon anyway, it will exit with a clear "another daemon holds the lease at " error.

docs.title: What happens when Obsidian and the daemon race on a write?
docs.text:
They don't, in practice:

  • Obsidian writes only to events/<cal>/<...>.md. The daemon also writes there, but only during reconcile of that specific file's series.
  • Writes are atomic (write-to-temp + rename). No reader sees a half-written file.
  • The daemon's self-write suppressor ignores fsnotify events for its own writes, so there's no ping-pong.

If Obsidian and the daemon both write the same file within ~ms of each other, the later write wins (fs semantics). Given the daemon only touches a file during a reconcile triggered by the root — and the root doesn't change that fast — the race window is tiny.

docs.title: What about .sync-conflict-* files?
docs.text: The daemon logs them at warn level and does not touch them. Resolve conflicts the way you normally would in a Syncthing vault: open both versions in Obsidian, pick one, delete the other. Once resolved, the daemon sees the surviving file on the next event.

docs.title: Can I have more than six calendars?
docs.text:
Yes. Edit the calendars: list in config.yaml, run calemdar setup to scaffold the new subfolder, and add a Full Calendar source in Obsidian pointing at it. Existing events in other calendars are untouched.

Removing a calendar is a schema change — move or delete its events first, then remove it from the list.

docs.title: Why is the timezone hardcoded?
docs.text: v1 is a personal tool for one person in one timezone. Multi-timezone correctness (DST transitions, per-event overrides, UTC storage vs local render) is a nontrivial chunk of work and not useful for the v1 user. If you need it, open an issue — the schema leaves room for a future tz: field.

docs.title: Where are notifications?
docs.text:
Shipped, in-daemon. Configure a ntfy URL + topic under notifications: in config.yaml, flip enabled: true, and the serve daemon starts pushing to <ntfy_url>/<ntfy_topic> whenever an upcoming event crosses one of the lead_minutes windows (default [5, 60]). All-day events are skipped (no natural trigger time).

Verify end-to-end before flipping the switch on the daemon:

calemdar notify test

Full reference: configuration.

No browser notifications. No per-event config. One topic, one lead-time list, optional per-calendar filter.

docs.title: I found a bug / want a feature
docs.text: Open an issue. Short reproducer, what you expected, what happened. For features, explain the use case before proposing an implementation — the simpler the feature the likelier it lands.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Zero to running daemon in five commands.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Go 1.22 or newer on your path.
  • An Obsidian vault you own (local disk; Syncthing-synced is fine).
  • The Full Calendar plugin installed in that vault.

docs.title: 1. Install the binary
docs.text:
go install github.com/arch-err/calemdar/cmd/calemdar@latest

This drops calemdar into $GOPATH/bin (usually ~/go/bin). Make sure that directory is on your $PATH.

Or build from source:

git clone https://github.com/arch-err/calemdar.git
cd calemdar
just install    # or: go install ./cmd/calemdar

AUR and Homebrew packages are not yet available. See the FAQ.

docs.title: 2. Write the config
docs.text:
calemdar config init

This writes ~/.config/calemdar/config.yaml with all defaults and drops you into $EDITOR. At minimum, set vault: to your Obsidian vault path:

vault: /home/you/obsidian/my-vault

Everything else has sane defaults. See Configuration for the full key list.

docs.title: 3. Scaffold the vault folders
docs.text:
calemdar setup

Creates recurring/, archive/, and events/<calendar>/ under the vault (or under base_path if set). Idempotent — safe to re-run. The daemon also runs this at startup.

docs.title: 4. Configure Full Calendar sources
docs.text:
In Obsidian, open Full Calendar settings and add one Local Calendar source per calendar subfolder under events/. Defaults are:

  • events/health
  • events/tech
  • events/work
  • events/life
  • events/friends-family
  • events/special

Give each a distinct colour. Do NOT add recurring/ or archive/ as sources — the daemon owns those.

docs.title: As a systemd user service (recommended)
docs.text:
cp examples/calemdar.service ~/.config/systemd/user/calemdar.service
systemctl --user daemon-reload
systemctl --user enable --now calemdar.service
journalctl --user -u calemdar -f    # watch it work

All tuning lives in config.yaml; the unit file takes no env or flags.

docs.title: Or run it in a terminal
docs.text:
calemdar serve

Ctrl-C to stop.

docs.title: First event
docs.text:
Create a recurring series interactively:

calemdar series new

calemdar writes the root to recurring/<slug>.md, then immediately expands it into concrete events under events/<calendar>/. Open Obsidian's Full Calendar and they appear on the grid.

To test the drag-safety: grab any expanded occurrence in Full Calendar and drop it on a new time. Open the file — user-owned: true is now in the frontmatter. The rest of the series is untouched, and future regenerations will leave this occurrence alone.

You can also create one-offs without a recurrence rule:

calemdar event new

docs.title: Next
docs.text:
• CLI reference for every subcommand.
  • Architecture for how reconcile, watcher, and store fit together.
  • FAQ for things that might trip you up.

docs.title: calemdar — frontmatter schema
docs.text: Locked v1. All frontmatter keys are stable — new keys may be added; existing keys will not change shape without a version bump.

docs.title: Recurring root
docs.text:
Location: recurring/<slug>.md. <slug> is human-chosen and cosmetic — identity is the UUID, not the filename. Rename at will.

Author: human (via editor or CLI). Server reads, never writes.

---
id: 019073c4-d7e0-7d8f-a1f3-8b2c9e5f4a10   # UUIDv7. stable identity. generate via `calemdar event new`
calendar: health                            # one of: health | tech | work | life | friends-family | special
title: "Workout"
start-date: 2026-05-01                      # first occurrence (YYYY-MM-DD)
until: 2027-05-01                           # optional. series ends on/before this date
start-time: "10:00"                         # HH:MM, 24h. omit for all-day
end-time: "11:00"                           # HH:MM, 24h. omit for all-day
all-day: false
freq: weekly                                # daily | weekly | monthly
interval: 1                                 # every N periods. 1 = every period
byday: [mon, wed, fri]                      # weekly only. lowercase three-letter weekday
bymonthday: [1, 15]                         # monthly only. optional. days of month
exceptions:                                 # occurrence dates to skip
  - 2026-05-08
  - 2026-06-24
---

Notes body is free-form markdown. Copied verbatim into each expanded event
at expansion time.

docs.title: v1 recurrence coverage
docs.text:
• freq: daily + interval: N — every N days from start-date.
  • freq: weekly + interval: N + byday: [...] — every N weeks on listed weekdays.
  • freq: monthly + interval: N + bymonthday: [...] — every N months on listed month-days.

Not v1: "last friday of month", "second weekday of month", count-based termination (use until instead). Add via full RFC 5545 RRULE when needed, not before.

docs.title: Expanded event
docs.text:
Location: events/<calendar>/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<slug>.md. Flat — Full Calendar's local-folder source does not recurse, so a year subfolder would hide events from its index.

Author: server (on series expansion) or human (for true one-offs, via CLI or editor). Human may drag/edit an expanded event in Obsidian — this flips user-owned: true and the server will not touch it again from root regen.

---
title: "Workout"
date: 2026-05-03
startTime: "10:00"
endTime: "11:00"
allDay: false
type: single                                 # FC native field. always "single" for calemdar
series-id: 019073c4-d7e0-7d8f-a1f3-8b2c9e5f4a10   # empty/absent = true one-off
series-expanded-at: 2026-04-24T11:00:00Z    # when server wrote this file (RFC 3339 UTC)
user-owned: false                            # true = server will not regenerate this
---

[[workout]]    # body-level backlink to root. human convenience, server ignores.

Notes body copied from root at expansion time.

docs.title: Field ownership
docs.text: field set by mutable by title server human (drag/edit in Obsidian) date server human (drag) startTime/endTime server human (drag/edit) allDay server human type server — (always single) series-id server — (immutable; breaks the link if changed) series-expanded-at server — user-owned server server (flipped to true on any human edit)

docs.title: One-offs
docs.text: A true one-off event has series-id absent or empty. Server ignores it for expansion logic; still subject to archive rule.

docs.title: Calendars
docs.text:
The six v1 calendars are stable strings. Each maps to a top-level folder under events/ and to a Full Calendar source with its own color.

  • health
  • tech
  • work
  • life
  • friends-family
  • special

Adding a calendar is a schema change (new subfolder, new FC source, new color). Removing one requires migrating or deleting its events.

docs.title: Identity
docs.text:
• Every recurring series has a UUIDv7 in its id field — stable across renames, immutable.
  • Expanded events reference the root by series-id, not by wikilink.
  • The wikilink in the body ([[slug]]) is a human convenience for Obsidian backlinks. The server ignores it.

docs.title: Timezones
docs.text: All times are in Europe/Stockholm for v1. Not stored per event. If multi-tz support is ever added, a tz: field is introduced and the default stays Europe/Stockholm.

docs.title: calemdar security review
docs.text: Commit: master @ /home/archerr/Git/arch-err/Tools/calemdar Threat model: single-user Linux daemon on its own files. No multi-tenant concerns.

docs.title: Executive summary
docs.text: Overall posture is good for the stated threat model. Path handling is defensive: slugs are sanitised to [a-z0-9-], calendars are validated against an allowlist before any EventPath call, dates must parse as YYYY-MM-DD before being substituted into filenames, and base_path rejects ... YAML parsing is yaml.v3 (has some built-in safeguards; no explicit depth/size caps beyond a 16MiB scanner buffer). The single most important finding is a credential-in-log leak in internal/notify: the ntfy URL is logged verbatim on every push, and the project's own documentation tells users to bake auth into the URL. No critical or high findings.

docs.title: Critical
docs.text: None.

docs.title: High
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Medium
docs.text: M1 — ntfy URL leaks basic-auth credentials to logs/stdout. internal/notify/notify.go:101, cmd/calemdar/cmd_notify.go:41. n.cfg.NtfyURL is written verbatim to log.Printf on every successful push (… → %s/%s"), and to stdout in notify test. The in-code comment at internal/notify/notify.go:107–108 explicitly instructs users to "bake auth into the URL" for private topics, so a https://user:pass@ntfy.example.com URL is a realistic config. Those creds then land in systemd-journal / tty scrollback. Fix: scrub userinfo before logging — parse with net/url and log u.Redacted() (stdlib, elides password). Applies to both call sites.

docs.title: Low
docs.text:
L1 — base_path validation allows absolute paths. internal/config/config.go:99–101. Validate only rejects ... An absolute base_path like /etc works in practice because filepath.Join(Root, "/etc") → Root/etc (Join does not honor absolute-path semantics on its non-first argument). Not exploitable today, but footgun-shaped — a future refactor that uses path.Clean+filepath.Rel or a different joiner could turn this into a traversal. Cheap to harden now. Fix: reject base_path starting with / (or any OS separator) and reject strings.HasPrefix(base_path, string(os.PathSeparator)).

L2 — NtfyTopic injected into URL path without validation or escaping. internal/notify/notify.go:119,143, internal/config/config.go:124–126. Topic is concatenated raw: TrimRight(NtfyURL,"/") + "/" + NtfyTopic. Config only requires non-empty/non-whitespace. Topic containing ../admin, ?query=1, or #frag would rewrite the POST target. Threat model is single-user so this is self-inflicted, but a shape check (^[A-Za-z0-9_-]{1,64}$ per ntfy's own allowed charset) is a one-liner that eliminates the surprise. Fix: validate topic regex in Notifications.Validate.

docs.title: Informational
docs.text:
I1 — YAML unmarshalling has no explicit resource caps. internal/model/parse.go:73,97, internal/fcparse/translate.go:27,44,61, internal/config/config.go:152. yaml.v3 is called on user-controlled file contents with no depth/size wrapper. The underlying library does cap alias expansion (mitigating classic billion-laughs), and the frontmatter is bounded by a 16MiB scanner buffer (parse.go:22, translate.go:225), so the practical blast radius is bounded memory on parse. Noted for the next reviewer; no action recommended under current threat model.

I2 — fsnotify self-write suppression can be spoofed by matching mtime. internal/watch/watch.go:290–311. isRecentSelfWrite suppresses if info.ModTime().Equal(m.mtime). An attacker with write access to the vault can call utimensat to match — file changes would be silently ignored by the daemon. Out of scope (requires local write access), but the mechanism is exactly the one the comment at watch.go:78–82 worries about, so documenting: yes, spoofable.

I3 — $EDITOR split by whitespace for config edit. cmd/calemdar/cmd_config.go:123–128. strings.Fields($EDITOR) then exec.Command(parts[0], parts[1:]...). Attacker controls their own env; noted as acknowledged per the brief. Not command-injection in the classic sense (no shell invoked), but values like EDITOR="vim --cmd ':!rm -rf ~'" would pass the option through to the editor. Same single-user caveat.

I4 — .calemdar/cache.db created with driver defaults (umask-dependent). internal/store/store.go:30–38. No explicit Chmod. On a default umask 022 this yields 0644. Fine for single-user, noted.

I5 — Unused-entry GC in selfWrites map. internal/watch/watch.go:63–64,135–146. Marks are deleted on TTL expiry only when something queries them. Marks for files that are never re-touched accumulate. Not a race; not a leak of security concern. Memory-bounded by workload. No action.

docs.title: Surfaces reviewed clean
docs.text:
• vault.Resolve, vault.EventPath, vault.Scaffold — all path construction rooted in validated or on-disk inputs; slug/calendar/date are either allowlisted or regex-constrained before joining.
  • fcparse.Slugify (internal/fcparse/slug.go) — output is strictly [a-z0-9-], no traversal payload possible.
  • reconcile.Series — writes through v.EventPath(r.Calendar, e.Date, r.Slug). r.Calendar is validated by expand.Expand (internal/expand/expand.go:24) before any path join; e.Date is parsed; r.Slug for disk-loaded roots is filepath.Base(path) which cannot contain separators.
  • reactor.migrateOne — calendar is derived from the on-disk relative path under events/, then re-validated against model.ValidCalendar; slug is fcparse.Slugify output; target existence is checked before write.
  • archive.Run — year is e.Date[:4] after ParseDate success; calendar is calendarFromPath derived from on-disk path under EventsDir.
  • cmd_event new / cmd_series new — slug from Slugify, calendar from askChoice(model.Calendars), date from ParseDate. No user string reaches a path unsanitised.
  • store/store.go — all SQL uses ? placeholders; ListUpcoming builds IN (...) with generated placeholders and args slice, no concat of user strings. All Query sites have matching defer rows.Close(). Connection is *sql.DB (pooled), Close() shuts it down.
  • store.Reindex — walks disk with filepath.WalkDir, calendar from on-disk path.
  • notify HTTP client — uses default Go http.Transport (TLS verification enabled), no custom TLSClientConfig, response body drained and closed on every path.
  • watch.Watcher — selfMu, pendingMu consistently guard their maps; the documented debounce-fire-time recheck at watch.go:249–251 closes the NotifySelfWrite-vs-raw-event race. flushPending runs under lock.
  • writer.WriteEvent / WriteRoot — path is caller-controlled but every caller routes through vetted path construction (see above). Mkdir + atomic-ish os.WriteFile.
